Q1: I tried to keep a pretty structured routine, honestly. I'd block out about three hours most weekdays after my morning classes to sit down with my readings and notes. Since I learn best through reading and writing, I'd rewrite key concepts and make detailed summaries. I aimed for around 22 hours a week, which felt manageable even with limited resources. Having that consistency helped me feel in control of my workload.
Q2: I definitely tried to stick to regular times, but I'd be lying if I said deadlines didn't shift things around. When assignments were due, I'd extend my evening sessions and cut back on other things. But generally, I liked having a predictable schedule because it reduced my stress. I found that when I let things get chaotic, I felt more scattered even if my stress levels stayed pretty low overall.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check for new announcements or to see if any readings had been posted. I was taking four online courses, so I needed to stay on top of updates across all of them. Sometimes I'd log in just to double-check due dates or to access the discussion boards. It became almost automatic—like checking email in the morning.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks, logging in became part of my daily rhythm. I'd check the LMS right after breakfast while having my coffee, just to see what was new. It wasn't forced; it just felt like a natural part of starting my study day. Having multiple online courses meant the LMS was basically my classroom, so it made sense to treat it that way.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were incredibly useful for me. I'm a read-write learner, so having access to PDFs and text-based content was perfect. I also found the discussion posts helpful because writing out my thoughts and reading others' perspectives reinforced what I was learning. The videos were okay, but I honestly preferred text I could highlight and annotate.
Q6: I'll admit I didn't watch all the videos thoroughly—I'd often skim through them or watch at double speed. With limited internet at home sometimes, downloading and streaming long videos was a hassle. I also skipped some of the optional supplementary materials when time got tight. It wasn't that they weren't valuable; I just had to prioritize what directly connected to assignments.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to an assignment or exam topic. If I finished something and felt like I understood a concept better or could write about it more confidently, that was satisfying. I also valued activities that let me practice applying knowledge rather than just passively consuming information. The sense of progress mattered a lot to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. With four courses and limited resources, I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy. Required activities had clear consequences, so they took priority. I'd sometimes circle back to optional stuff if I had extra time, but honestly, that was rare. It's not that I didn't want to do them—I just had to make choices.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could to study offline. My internet isn't always reliable, so I'd save PDFs, lecture notes, and even transcripts of videos when available. I'd spend hours working through my printed notes and handwritten summaries at my desk without being online at all. A lot of my actual learning happened with just paper and a highlighter.
Q10: Most of my deep studying happened offline with downloaded materials and my own written notes. I'd spend entire afternoons rewriting concepts, making flashcards, and testing myself—none of which showed up in the LMS. Sometimes I'd read the same PDF five times over a week, but the system only registered that initial download. My engagement was way deeper than my login patterns suggested.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a reading or lecture notes once, then work with that material for days without going back online. I'd print things out when I could afford to, or just work from my laptop offline. The LMS would show one access, but that material might be the foundation of a whole week's worth of studying for me. It was more efficient that way given my situation.
Q12: There were moments, especially mid-semester, when juggling four online courses got overwhelming. I remember one week when three assignments overlapped, and I felt stretched thin even though my stress stayed manageable. Surprisingly, I didn't feel anxious—more just surprised by how quickly things piled up. My resilience kicked in, and I just powered through, but engagement definitely dipped during those crunch times.
Q13: Fatigue was probably the biggest factor. Studying 22 hours a week across multiple courses while managing limited resources took a toll sometimes. When I was tired, I'd log in and just go through the motions—checking boxes rather than really absorbing material. Competing responsibilities like budgeting my time and resources also meant I couldn't always engage as deeply as I wanted to.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through discussion posts or skimming readings, but my mind was elsewhere. I was physically present but not really processing anything meaningful. It usually happened when I was tired or had been studying for too long. I'd realize I'd read the same paragraph three times without retaining anything.
Q15: I postponed some of the heavier readings when I felt overwhelmed, even knowing they were important for exams. I think I convinced myself I'd get to them later, but sometimes later never came. It wasn't procrastination from laziness—more like strategic avoidance when I felt I couldn't give something proper attention. Looking back, that might have hurt my exam performance.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for LMS activity, honestly. I'd check in more frequently as due dates approached, making sure I hadn't missed anything. My login frequency definitely spiked around assignment submissions. Without deadlines, I might have let things slide more, so in a way, they kept me accountable even if the pressure was sometimes uncomfortable.
Q17: Feedback motivated me to engage more when it was specific and constructive. If I got comments that showed the instructor actually read my work, I'd be more invested in the next assignment. Generic feedback didn't do much for me. After the CBT session I had, I also became more aware of how I responded to criticism and tried to see feedback as helpful rather than discouraging.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. When modules were clearly organized with explicit expectations, I knew exactly what to focus on. I appreciated having a roadmap. But when things were scattered or the connection between materials and assessments was unclear, I felt like I was guessing at what mattered. Clear structure made it easier to plan my limited study time effectively.
Q19: Having text-based resources readily available made engagement easier for me as a read-write learner. What made it harder was when important content was locked behind long videos with no transcripts. Also, discussion boards were helpful, but the interface could be clunky. When the design matched my learning style, I thrived; when it didn't, I had to work around it.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work that meets expectations. They could be reading everything, watching videos, participating in discussions, but still feel uncertain about how to demonstrate their learning. Or maybe they're dealing with things outside of school that make it hard to follow through. High activity doesn't always mean clarity or confidence.
Q21: That actually resonates with me somewhat. I studied extensively offline, so my LMS activity didn't fully reflect my effort. Someone could download materials once, study intensively from those files, and perform decently without logging in constantly. The LMS captures access, not absorption. I think I fell into this category, though my exam score didn't reflect my effort, which still surprises me.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when students log in, what they click on, submission times. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't capture whether someone actually understood what they read or just skimmed it. They also miss offline studying, mental effort, and the personal circumstances that shape how someone learns. It's like measuring the tip of an iceberg.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, making connections, and caring about understanding it—not just completing tasks. It's when I find myself curious about a topic beyond what's required, or when I'm genuinely invested in doing well. Engagement is mental and emotional involvement, not just showing up or clicking through content. It's about being present in my learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent rewriting notes by hand, or the hours I studied from printed materials without internet. It wouldn't capture the CBT session that helped me manage my approach to challenges, or how I maintained a positive mood despite my exam results being disappointing. My resilience, my offline dedication, my actual thought process—none of that appears in the data.
